content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Alison C. Neufeld 
Liebert Cassidy Whitmore 
!53 Townsend Street, Suite 520 
San Francisco, California 94107 
July 18, 2011 
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-11-125 
Dear Ms. Neufeld: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Hartnell College Board of 
Trustees regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 
1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not 
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of 
interest or Government Code Section 1090. Finally, we do not provide third-party advice or 
advice regarding past conduct. Therefore, our advice pertains solely to your duty to advise the 
Board regarding the application of the Act's conflict of interest provision to future actions by the 
Hartnell College Board of Trustees. 
Because you are do not appear to be representing Mr. Freeman, but rather the Hartnell 
College Board of Trustees, we are providing informal assistance. 2 
QUESTION 
Does Hartnell College Board of Trustees member Bill Freeman have a conflict of interest 
in participating in a governmental decision to approve a collective bargaining agreement that 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; regulation l8329(c)(3), copy enclosed). 


File No. I-11-125 
Page No. 2 
would establish salaries for the both the person who owns the house in which he lives and for the 
person with whom he shares the living quarters? 
CONCLUSION 
Mr. Freeman has a conflict of interest in participating in a governmental decision 
regarding a collective bargaining agreement only if it can be established that either the person 
who owns the house in which he resides or the person who shares living arrangements with him 
is a source of gift to him in the amount of $420 or more over the 12-month period before the 
governmental decision takes place. 
FACTS 
Mr. Freeman is an elected member of the Hartnell College Board of Trustees (the 
"Board"). Mr. Freeman currently resides in a house owned by a faculty member employed by 
Hartnell College, who rents the house but does not live there. Mr. Freeman has a roommate, who 
is also a faculty member employed by Hartnell College. Both the landlord and the roommate are 
represented by the Hartnell College Faculty Association (the "HCFA"). 
Mr. Freeman has informed Hartnell College that he pays rent in the amount of $540 per 
month and occupies one bedroom in the house. He stated that he makes his rent payments in 
cash. Mr. Freeman's roommate stated that he pays $600 rent per month and occupies two of the 
bedrooms. He makes his rent payment by check. This housing arrangement has existed for 
approximately a year. 
The combined monthly rent of $1,140 paid by Mr. Freeman and his roommate is below 
the $1,650 to $1,800 estimated fair market value rental range for a house in the neighborhood. 
Mr. Freeman's roommate pays for utilities, cable TV, Internet and lawn services at the 
house. The roommate states that he collects Mr. Freeman's half of those expenses when and if 
he remembers to ask Mr. Freeman for those payments. The roommate does not always 
remember to ask for Mr. Freeman's contribution to these shared expenses. 
Although you have not inquired about any specific governmental decision, your proposed 
analysis states that "Mr. Freeman regularly votes upon matters within the Board's jurisdiction, 
including the adoption or rejection of the collective bargaining agreement between the District 
and HCFA." 
ANALYSIS 
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8100l(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 


File No. 1-11-125 
Page No. 3 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, 
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental 
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her 
financial interests. 
It is clear from your inquiry that Mr. Freeman is a public official (Step 1, Regulation 
18701) engaging in a governmental decision (Step 2, Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.4). 
Your specific concern is whether he has an economic interest (Step 3, Regulations 18703 and 
18703 .4) as a source of gift in either his roommate or the proferty owner of the house in which 
he lives as a result of a possible discount on the rent he pays. 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including: 
• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or 
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.l(a)); 
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any 
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.l(b).) 
• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect 
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, 
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) 
• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to 
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); 
Regulation 18703.4.) 
• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her 
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18703.5.) 
3 A gift is defined as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and included a rebate or discount in the price of anything of 
mlue unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without 
regard to official status." (Section 82028, Emphasis added.) 


File No. I-11-125 
Page No. 4 
The individuals described would be considered "directly involved" in the governmental 
decision (Step 4, Regulation 18704.1), as "subjects of' the collective bargaining agreement 
because the agreement involves the issuance of an "entitlement to or contract with" them. 
Therefore, any financial effect on these individuals would be presumed to be material. (Step 5, 
Regulations 18705 and 18705.4.) Moreover, the material financial effect would be reasonably 
foreseeable. (Step 6, Regulation 18706.)4 
Therefore, the sole question as to whether or not Mr. Freeman would have a conflict of 
interest in participating in a Board decision that establishes salaries for the individuals in 
question turns on whether or not he had received a gift in the amount of $420 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. 
Unfortunately, the facts you have provided are somewhat ambiguous. For example, you 
have provided no information as to any rental agreement between the owner of the house and 
Mr. Freeman. It appears the house may be leased from the owner by Mr. Freeman's roommate 
who then sublets him a room for $540 per month. The facts almost suggest that this is the more 
likely scenario as Mr. Freeman pays his rent in cash while the roommate pays by check, and the 
utilities and maintenance are handled by the roommate. If such is the case, the discount in 
market value would be a benefit to Mr. Freeman's roommate and not to Mr. Freeman. 
You have not provided any information regarding the fair market rental value of a room 
in a house in this neighborhood, so we are unable to determine if Mr. Freeman is receiving a 
discounted benefit on his rent to the roommate. 
We can advise that if Mr. Freeman is, in fact, obligated to pay half of the utility and 
service charges above his monthly rental payment, whatever amount that his roommate has 
waived would be considered a gift, and if the amount aggregates to $420 or more over the 12-
month period before the governmental decision regarding the collective bargaining agreement is 
made, Mr. Freeman would have a conflict of interest participating in that decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
WJL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
J n W. Wallace 
A istant General Counsel 
,-
William J. Lenkeit -rd­
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
4 Steps seven and eight involve the "public generally" and "legally required" exceptions to the prohibition 
against participating in a governmental decision when a conflict of interest is established. Because you have not 
provided any information to suggest the either of these two exceptions may be applicable, we have not included 
them in our analysis.
